title: SEGMENT 10 — Designing milasen — eight candidates, screening in patient fibroblasts, picking TY777

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Designing milasen — eight candidates, screening in patient fibroblasts, picking TY777
Welcome back.
Before the break we talked about the precedent.
Now let's talk about the actual design of the drug.
Yu's team had, by the spring and summer of 2017, a clear molecular picture.
They knew the second mutation was an SVA in intron 6 of MFSD8.
They knew the SVA had introduced a cryptic splice acceptor, called i6.
SA, about 119 bases upstream of the real exon 7 acceptor.
They knew the cryptic acceptor was being used in preference to the real one, producing a mis-spliced transcript with a premature stop codon.
And they knew what they wanted to do — they wanted to design a splice-modulating ASO that would bind to the region around the cryptic acceptor and prevent the spliceosome from using it.
How do you actually design such a molecule?
You start by considering the region around the cryptic acceptor, and you identify all the sequence motifs that the splicing machinery cares about.
The cryptic acceptor itself is one such motif.
Adjacent to it are likely to be other sequences called exonic splicing enhancers — short motifs that recruit splicing factors to nearby splice sites and increase their utilization.

If you can mask the cryptic acceptor and the nearby exonic splicing enhancers, you weaken the cryptic splicing event in multiple ways at once, and you tip the balance back toward the real exon 7 acceptor being used.
The team designed eight different candidate ASOs, each one targeted to a slightly different region of the cryptic acceptor and its surroundings.

Each candidate was a short — eighteen to twenty-two nucleotides — piece of 2'-MOE phosphorothioate nucleic acid, with a slightly different sequence chosen to base-pair with a slightly different stretch of the MFSD8 pre-mRNA around the SVA insertion.

The reason for designing eight candidates rather than one is that the precise position of an ASO along a target sequence often matters a great deal for its efficacy — sometimes a shift of just a few bases can change a useless ASO into an active one or vice versa, because of factors like local RNA secondary structure, the precise position of the binding site relative to splicing factor binding sites, and so on.
So the standard approach is to design several candidates, screen them empirically, and pick the best one.
To screen them, the team needed Mila's own cells.

Specifically, they needed cells from Mila that carried both her mutations and that were producing the broken splicing pattern, so that the team could measure whether each candidate ASO actually corrected the splicing in cells of Mila's specific genotype.

And the standard cell type for this kind of work is the fibroblast — a kind of cell that lives in your skin and connective tissue, that grows readily in tissue culture, and that can be obtained from a routine skin biopsy.
In May of 2017, fibroblasts were established from Mila's skin biopsy and grown in culture.

The team confirmed that those fibroblasts were producing the abnormal, mis-spliced MFSD8 transcripts — about ninety-two percent of the transcripts from her SVA-carrying allele were going through the cryptic acceptor and including the unwanted intronic sequence.
Only about eight percent were being spliced correctly, exon 6 to exon 7.
That baseline of eight percent correct splicing represented essentially the residual leaky output of the SVA-carrying allele, plus whatever transcription was happening from the c.1102G>C allele.
Now the team did what was, in concept, a simple experiment.

They added each of the eight candidate ASOs to dishes of Mila's fibroblasts, at various concentrations, let the cells take the ASO up and process the RNA for a couple of days, and then measured what fraction of the MFSD8 transcripts in the treated cells were being spliced correctly — exon 6 directly to exon 7 — versus what fraction were still going through the cryptic acceptor.
One of the eight candidates was clearly the winner.
It was called, in the lab's internal nomenclature, TY777.
It was a twenty-two nucleotide oligonucleotide.
In Mila's fibroblasts, treatment with TY777 tripled the proportion of correctly spliced MFSD8 transcripts, from a baseline of about eight percent to approximately thirty-four percent.
The half-maximal effective concentration — the dose at which half of the maximum splicing correction was achieved — was in the nanomolar range, which is a very respectable potency for a clinical ASO.
TY777, ultimately renamed milasen, was the molecule they would advance toward the patient.
The team did not stop with measuring splicing correction.
They went on to measure whether the splicing correction translated into rescue of the actual cellular phenotypes of the disease.
Remember — the underlying problem in Mila's cells is lysosomal dysfunction.

The lysosomes accumulate undigested material, they swell into abnormal vacuoles, they fluoresce a particular way under the microscope because of accumulated lipofuscin pigment, and the cellular process called autophagy — by which cells deliver internal waste to lysosomes for breakdown — gets jammed up because the lysosomes can't process what's being delivered to them.
In Mila's fibroblasts, treatment with TY777 rescued all of these phenotypes.
The abnormal lysosomal vacuoles got smaller and less numerous.
The autofluorescence — the lipofuscin accumulation — went down.
The autophagic flux — the rate at which material moved through the autophagy pathway — normalized.
The cellular machinery, with about thirty-four percent functional MFSD8 transcripts being made, was producing enough working transporter to begin clearing the lysosomal backlog.

This is the moment in the paper where the team had concrete molecular and cellular evidence that the drug they were about to make was actually doing something useful, in cells from the actual patient who would receive it.
The splicing was corrected.
The downstream lysosomal phenotypes were rescued.
The half-maximal dose was reasonable.
The candidate had been selected.
By the fall of 2017 — October — the in-vitro validation was complete.
Now they had to take this oligonucleotide out of the dish and put it into a living animal.
And then, eventually, into a human child.
